from [[Texas]] ==Impact of demographic changes== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 election.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[Real Clear Politics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Real Clear Politics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|authors=Frey, William H; Teixeira, Ruy; Griffin, Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==In popular culture== * In [[Leslie vs. April|an episode]] of the NBC television sitcom "[[Parks and Recreation]]", character [[Leslie Knope]] reveals she plans to stand in the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|title=Vice President Joe Biden Breaks Hearts on Parks and Recreation—Watch the Video|url=http://www.eonline.com/news/362788/vice-president-joe-biden-breaks-hearts-on-parks-and-recreation-watch-the-video|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=E! Online|date=3 November 2012}} * In the 2015 series finale of the FOX musical comedy series "[[Glee (TV series)|Glee]]", character [[Sue Sylvester]] announces she plans to stand in the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|last1=Hinckley|first1=David|title='Glee' finale flashes back to the start, then forward to the 'New Directions' future|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/tv/glee-finale-flashes-back-future-article-1.2157655|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=[[New York Daily News]]|date=19 March 2015}} *A 2008 feminist advocacy documentary by [[Amy Sewell]], "What's Your Point Honey?", follows the lives of seven women selected as part of a 16-year plan to "groom" potential candidates for the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|last1=Finn|first1=Robin|title=A Knack for Putting Feminism on Film|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/05/30/nyregion/30lives.html?_r=0|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=[[New York Times]]|date=30 May 2008}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]]

{{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. ==Nominations== There are no current nominations. ====Potential candidates==== {{div col|colwidth=30em}} * [[Justin Amash]], independent Representative from Michigan {{cite web|url=https://www.thelibertyconservative.com/report-is-justin-amash-considering-a-2020-primary-challenge-to-trump/|title=Report: Is Justin Amash considering a 2020 primary challenge to Trump?|date=9 August 2017|publisher=The Liberty Conservative}}{{cite web|url=http://thehill.com/homenews/campaign/345656-the-five-kinds-of-republicans-who-could-primary-trump|title=The five kinds of Republicans who could primary Trump|first=Ian|last=Swanson|date=7 August 2017|publisher=The Hill}}{{dead link|date=January 2019}}{{efn|Amash is not a member of the Republican Party, but has been the subject of speculation as a potential Republican Party candidate.|group=}} * [[Kelly Ayotte]], former Senator from New Hampshire {{cite web|url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2017/11/03/will-america-have-woman-president-politics-2017-215769|title=Will America Ever Have a Woman President?|publisher=}} * [[Diane Black]], former Representative from Tennessee * [[Marsha Blackburn]], Senator from Tennessee * [[Scott Brown (politician)|Scott Brown]], Ambassador to New Zealand[https://fiscalnote.com/2014/07/16/presidential-politics-part-1-2020-2024]{{dead link|date=January 2019}} * [[George P. from [[Texas]] ==Impact of demographic changes== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 election.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[Real Clear Politics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Real Clear Politics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|authors=Frey, William H; Teixeira, Ruy; Griffin, Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==In popular culture== * In [[Leslie vs. April|an episode]] of the NBC television sitcom "[[Parks and Recreation]]", character [[Leslie Knope]] reveals she plans to stand in the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|title=Vice President Joe Biden Breaks Hearts on Parks and Recreation—Watch the Video|url=http://www.eonline.com/news/362788/vice-president-joe-biden-breaks-hearts-on-parks-and-recreation-watch-the-video|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=E! Online|date=3 November 2012}} * In the 2015 series finale of the FOX musical comedy series "[[Glee (TV series)|Glee]]", character [[Sue Sylvester]] announces she plans to stand in the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|last1=Hinckley|first1=David|title='Glee' finale flashes back to the start, then forward to the 'New Directions' future|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/tv/glee-finale-flashes-back-future-article-1.2157655|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=[[New York Daily News]]|date=19 March 2015}} *A 2008 feminist advocacy documentary by [[Amy Sewell]], "What's Your Point Honey?", follows the lives of seven women selected as part of a 16-year plan to "groom" potential candidates for the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|last1=Finn|first1=Robin|title=A Knack for Putting Feminism on Film|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/05/30/nyregion/30lives.html?_r=0|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=[[New York Times]]|date=30 May 2008}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]]

{{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. ==Nominations== There are no current nominations. ====Potential candidates==== {{div col|colwidth=30em}} * [[Kelly Ayotte]], former Senator from New Hampshire {{cite web|url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2017/11/03/will-america-have-woman-president-politics-2017-215769|title=Will America Ever Have a Woman President?|publisher=}} * [[Diane Black]], former Representative from Tennessee * [[Marsha Blackburn]], Senator from Tennessee * [[Scott Brown (politician)|Scott Brown]], Ambassador to New Zealand[https://fiscalnote.com/2014/07/16/presidential-politics-part-1-2020-2024]{{dead link|date=January 2019}} * [[George P. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|authors=Frey, William H; Teixeira, Ruy; Griffin, Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==In popular culture== * In [[Leslie vs. April|an episode]] of the NBC television sitcom "[[Parks and Recreation]]", character [[Leslie Knope]] reveals she plans to stand in the 2024 U.S. presidential election.{{cite news|title=Vice President Joe Biden Breaks Hearts on Parks and Recreation—Watch the Video|url=http://www.eonline.com/news/362788/vice-president-joe-biden-breaks-hearts-on-parks-and-recreation-watch-the-video|accessdate=6 February 2016|work=E! The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]).

[[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.<ref name="va">{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}}</ref> At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024.<ref name="va"/>

==Nominations==
There are no current nominations.

Potential Candidate:

Mike Pence, Vice President of the United States, Former Governor of Indiana, Former U.S. Rep. Indiana's 6th Congressional District

Nikki Haley, Former U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations, Former Governor of South Carolina

John Thune, U.S. Senator from South Dakota, Former U.S. Rep. South Dakota at large Congressional District

Donald Trump Jr, Businessman, Son of President Donald Trump 

Ted Cruz, U.S. Senator from Texas, Presidential Candidate in 2016

Marco Rubio, U.S. Senator from Florida, Presidential Candidate in 2016

Mike Pompeo, U.S. Secretary of State, Former Director of the Central Intelligence Agency, Former U.S. Rep. Kansas 4th Congressional District

George P. Bush, Texas Land Commissioner, Son of Former Florida Governor and 2016 Presidential Candidate Jeb Bush

==Impact of demographic changes==
The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].<ref>{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}}</ref> This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 election.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}}</ref>

In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}}</ref>

{{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}}

In a separate analysis for [[Real Clear Politics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Real Clear Politics]]|date=December 30, 2013}}</ref>

Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."<ref>{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}}</ref>

A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.<ref>{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}}</ref>

{{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. of [[New York (state)|New York]]}} ===Libertarian Party=== ===Green Party=== ===Independents=== ==Impact of demographic changes== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 election.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]] }}

However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]] }} [[Category:Draft articles]]

from [[New York (state)|New York]]}} ===Libertarian Party=== ===Green Party=== ===Independents=== ==Impact of demographic changes== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 election.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]] }} [[Category:Draft articles]]

But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=brookings.org|publisher=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:2024]] [[:Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[:Category:United States presidential elections by date|2024]] }} [[Category:Draft articles]]

[[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); Gossip Cop can debunk it.}} ===Libertarian Party=== ===Green Party=== ===Independents=== ==See also== *[[2024 United States elections]] *[[2024 United States gubernatorial elections]] *[[2024 United States House of Representatives elections]] *[[2024 United States Senate elections]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Wikiquote}} {{2024 United States presidential election}} {{2024 United States elections}} {{United States presidential elections}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:United States presidential election, 2020}} {{draft categories| [[Category:2024 United States presidential election| ]] [[Category:History of the United States (1991–present)]] [[Category:Contemporary history of the United States]] }} [[Category:Draft articles]]

{{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present);
