[[User:Koopinator|Koopinator]] ([[User talk:Koopinator|talk]]) 09:04, 24 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Reywas92|Reywas92]][[User talk:Reywas92|Talk]] 00:44, 25 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=1133602304|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today’s race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Numberguy6|Numberguy6]] ([[User talk:Numberguy6|talk]]) 01:13, 26 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Numberguy6|Numberguy6]] ([[User talk:Numberguy6|talk]]) 01:13, 26 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again for the 2032 and 2036 elections.{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of Sun Belt states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible rejoinder, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased minority turnout could shift solidly Republican states toward the "swing" category, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Numberguy6|Numberguy6]] ([[User talk:Numberguy6|talk]]) 18:51, 29 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Impact of demographic changes=== The election has been the early topic of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], occurring as a result of the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election before being reapportioned again after the [[2030 United States Census]].{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of [[Sun Belt]] states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that traditionally support Republican candidates during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though geography has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40-percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." As a possible counterbalance, however, the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|accessdate=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased [[Voter turnout#Demographics|minority turnout]] could shift solidly Republican states towards being [[swing state]]s, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Numberguy6|Numberguy6]] ([[User talk:Numberguy6|talk]]) 18:51, 29 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Effects of demographic changes=== The election has been the early subject of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], which will follow the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election. Reapportionment will be conducted again after the [[2030 United States Census]].{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'', [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of [[Sun Belt]] states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that have supported Republican candidates since the late 20th century, during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though this demographic shift to the South and Southwest has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40 percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." But the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|access-date=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased [[Voter turnout#Demographics|minority turnout]] could shift solidly Republican states toward being [[swing state]]s, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Numberguy6|Numberguy6]] ([[User talk:Numberguy6|talk]]) 18:51, 29 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Effects of demographic changes=== The election has been the early subject of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], which will follow the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election. Reapportionment will be conducted again after the [[2030 United States Census]].{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'' (2009), [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of [[Sun Belt]] states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that have supported Republican candidates since the late 20th century, during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though this demographic shift to the South and Southwest has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40 percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." But the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|access-date=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased [[Voter turnout#Demographics|minority turnout]] could shift solidly Republican states toward being [[swing state]]s, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} By the end of May 2020, the [[COVID-19 pandemic]] had killed at least 100,000 persons in the United States, made more than 1.6 million persons in the U.S. ill, caused record-breaking unemployment as millions lost their jobs when businesses and other enterprises closed in an effort to mitigate contagion, and had wide, adverse economic effects across the United States. While the peak seems to have passed in some locales, others are having surges of new cases and deaths, even as states try to restart their economies in various forms. Job losses and application for unemployment benefits are at record levels, and major national businesses have closed or are in bankruptcy. The political and economic effects of the pandemic, which is still running, in the near term or in 2024 cannot yet be assessed.{{citation needed|date=May 2020}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Greg Abbott]], [[Governor of Texas]] (2015–present); [[User:Devonian Wombat|Devonian Wombat]] ([[User talk:Devonian Wombat|talk]]) 00:58, 31 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Effects of demographic changes=== The election has been the early subject of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], which will follow the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election. Reapportionment will be conducted again after the [[2030 United States Census]].{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'' (2009), [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of [[Sun Belt]] states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that have supported Republican candidates since the late 20th century, during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though this demographic shift to the South and Southwest has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40 percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." But the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|access-date=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased [[Voter turnout#Demographics|minority turnout]] could shift solidly Republican states toward being [[swing state]]s, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} By the end of May 2020, the [[COVID-19 pandemic]] had killed at least 100,000 persons in the United States, made more than 1.6 million persons in the U.S. ill, caused record-breaking unemployment as millions lost their jobs when businesses and other enterprises closed in an effort to mitigate contagion, and had wide, adverse economic effects across the United States. While the peak seems to have passed in some locales, others are having surges of new cases and deaths, even as states try to restart their economies in various forms. Job losses and application for unemployment benefits are at record levels, and major national businesses have closed or are in bankruptcy. The political and economic effects of the pandemic, which is still running, in the near term or in 2024 cannot yet be assessed.{{citation needed|date=May 2020}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Tom Cotton]], [[United States Senate|U.S. Senator]] from [[Arkansas]] (2015–present); [[User:Devonian Wombat|Devonian Wombat]] ([[User talk:Devonian Wombat|talk]]) 00:58, 31 May 2020 (UTC)}} {{Infobox election | election_name = 2024 United States presidential election | country = United States | type = presidential | opinion_polls = | ongoing = yes | previous_election = 2020 United States presidential election | previous_year = ''2020'' | next_election = 2028 United States presidential election | next_year = ''2028'' | election_date = November 5, 2024 | flag_year = | votes_for_election = | needed_votes = | image1 = | nominee1 = | party1 = | home_state1 = | popular_vote1 = | percentage1 = | image2 = | nominee2 = | party2 = | home_state2 = | popular_vote2 = | percentage2 = | map_image = | map_size = | map_caption = | title = President | before_election = TBD | before_party = | after_party = | after_election = TBD }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} The '''2024 United States presidential election''' will be the 60th quadrennial U.S. presidential election. It will be the first presidential election to be run with population data from the [[2020 United States Census|2020 census]] ([[Draft:2024 United States presidential election#Impact of demographic changes|see below for details]]). [[United States presidential election|U.S. presidential elections]] are scheduled on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. Following that schedule, the 2024 elections are to be held on Tuesday, November 5, 2024.{{cite web|title=Election Planning Calendar|url=http://www.essex-virginia.org/UserFiles/Servers/Server_62876/File/Voter%20Registration/Election%20Planning%20Calendar%20Through%202024%20%28ESSEX%29.pdf|website=essex-virginia.org|publisher=[[Essex County, Virginia]]|accessdate=6 February 2016}} At present, general elections follow caucuses and primary elections held to determine the nominees of the major parties. According to an election calendar of [[Essex County, Virginia]], Virginia has scheduled a [[presidential primary]] for March 5, 2024. The winner of the 2024 presidential election is scheduled to be inaugurated on January 20, 2025. ==Background== ===Effects of demographic changes=== The election has been the early subject of attention by analysts and commentators, as it will be the first U.S. presidential election to occur after the [[United States congressional apportionment#Reapportionment|reapportionment]] of votes in the [[United States Electoral College]], which will follow the [[2020 United States Census]].{{cite news|last1=McArdle|first1=Megan|title=2016 Might Look Safe to Democrats. But 2024?|url=http://www.bloombergview.com/articles/2015-05-21/2016-might-look-safe-to-democrats-but-2024-|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[Bloomberg L.P.|Bloomberg]]|date=May 21, 2015}}{{cite book|last1=Janda|first1=Kenneth|title=The Challenge of Democracy: American Government in Global Politics|date=2013|publisher=Wadsworth|isbn=978-1133602309|page=218}} This realignment of electoral college votes will remain consistent through the 2028 election. Reapportionment will be conducted again after the [[2030 United States Census]].{{cite book|last1=Neale|first1=Thomas|title=The Electoral College: How It Works in Contemporary Presidential Elections|date=2012|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|url=https://www.fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL32611.pdf|accessdate=October 30, 2015}} In a study of the implications of redistribution of electors in 2024 published in ''[[Perspectives on Politics]]'' (2009), [[University of Georgia]] political scientist Edward Burmila concluded that:{{cite journal|last1=Burmila|first1=Edward|title=The Electoral College after Census 2010 and 2020: The Political Impact of Population Growth and Redistribution|journal=[[Perspectives on Politics]]|date=December 2009|volume=7|issue=4|pages=837–847|doi=10.1017/S1537592709991836|url=http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=6677188}} {{quote|Democratic presidential candidates will find it increasingly difficult to win elections without having some success in the South and Southwest as Barack Obama did in 2008 but many previous candidates failed to do. While illegal immigration will also benefit some solid Democratic states such as California, on balance Republican presidential candidates are poised to benefit from the status of [[Sun Belt]] states as magnets for both foreign immigration and domestic migration from a retirement cohort of unprecedented size.}} In a separate analysis for [[RealClearPolitics]], Sean Trende predicted a less significant movement of three electoral votes from traditionally Democratic Party states to those that have supported Republican candidates since the late 20th century, during the redistribution of electors that will occur for the 2024 election.{{cite news|last1=Trende|first1=Sean|title=Population Data Show More Movement South and West|url=http://www.realclearpolitics.com/articles/2013/12/30/population_data_show_more_movement_south_and_west.html|accessdate=October 30, 2015|work=[[RealClearPolitics]]|date=December 30, 2013}} Though this demographic shift to the South and Southwest has been said to favor a Republican candidate in the 2024 election, some have noted that demographic trends may help a Democratic candidate if current group party affinities continue to hold in 2024. [[Ethnic minorities]] are predicted to constitute 40 percent of eligible voters age 30 to 44, a constituency described as "ripe for Democratic retention." But the unusually large [[baby boomers|baby boomer generation]] will have mostly entered the 65+ age range by 2024. According to the [[Brookings Institution]], these older voters "will be easier for the Republican Party to retain if current generational voting affinities continue."{{cite web|title=Today's race and generational voting preferences cannot predict future election outcomes|url=http://www.brookings.edu/blogs/the-avenue/posts/2015/06/01-race-generational-voting-elections-frey|website=[[Brookings Institution]]|access-date=October 30, 2015|last1=Frey|first1=William H.|date=June 1, 2015}} A report by the [[American Enterprise Institute]] also predicts that the aging white population could result in an increase in Republican support, leading to a Republican lead in the 2020 and 2024 elections, but that this will be followed by Democratic advantages in 2028 and 2032. However, the report also suggests that increased [[Voter turnout#Demographics|minority turnout]] could shift solidly Republican states toward being [[swing state]]s, overcoming the shift of the aging population and resulting in a Democratic win in 2024.{{Citation|title=America's Electoral Future|date=Feb 2016|url=https://www.aei.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/SOC2016-report.pdf|last1=Frey|first1=William H|last2=Teixeira|first2=Ruy|last3=Griffin|first3=Robert|series=States of Change|publisher=American Enterprise Institute}} By the end of May 2020, the [[COVID-19 pandemic]] had killed at least 100,000 persons in the United States, made more than 1.6 million persons in the U.S. ill, caused record-breaking unemployment as millions lost their jobs when businesses and other enterprises closed in an effort to mitigate contagion, and had wide, adverse economic effects across the United States. While the peak seems to have passed in some locales, others are having surges of new cases and deaths, even as states try to restart their economies in various forms. Job losses and application for unemployment benefits are at record levels, and major national businesses have closed or are in bankruptcy. The political and economic effects of the pandemic, which is still running, in the near term or in 2024 cannot yet be assessed.{{citation needed|date=May 2020}} ==Nominations== ===Republican Party=== {{further information|2024 Republican Party presidential primaries}} ====Potential candidates==== * [[Tom Cotton]], [[United States Senate|U.S. Senator]] from [[Arkansas]] (2015–present); [[United States House of Representatives|U.S. Representative]] from [[Arkansas' 4th congressional district|AR-04]] (2013–2015){{cite web|url=https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/news/campaigns/its-never-too-early-tom-cotton-headed-to-new-hampshire-to-put-a-toe-in-2024-waters|title='It's never too early': Tom Cotton headed to New Hampshire to put toe in 2024 waters|website=Washington Examiner|last1=Drucker|first1=David M.|date=March 3, 2020|accessdate=May 30, 2020}}{{cite web|url=https://www.bloombergquint.com/gadfly/tom-cotton-op-ed-on-sending-troops-is-first-salvo-in-2024-race|title= Tom Cotton Declares His Candidacy for 2024|website=Bloomberg Quint|last1=Wilkinson|first1=Francis|date=June 4, 2020|accessdate=June 5, 2020}} * [[Ron DeSantis]], [[Governor of Florida]] (2019–present); [[United States House of Representatives|U.S. Representative]] from [[Florida's 6th congressional district|FL-06]] (2013–2018){{cite web|url=https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2020/05/22/florida-republicans-2024-trump-desantis-rubio-scott-gaetz-273077|title=The Presidential Race Florida Is Really Talking About|website=Politico|last1=Dixon|first1=Matt|date=May 22, 2020|accessdate=May 27, 2020}}{{cite web|url=https://www.orlandosentinel.com/opinion/scott-maxwell-commentary/os-op-2024-desantis-rubio-gaetz-rick-scott-maxwell-20200527-lolp5qmph5dfnnhorqbf4ck4x4-story.html|title=A ‘Florida Man’ primary in 2024?
